name: Multimodal Health Diagnostics App
short: A multimodal health platform ingesting wearable signals, blood reports, and chat history — processed through a multi-model AI architecture to deliver personalised diagnostics and recommendations. Built and shipped end-to-end as a single engineer.
problem: Personal health context was fragmented across wearable apps, lab PDFs, and patient chats. Nothing talked to anything; clinicians and users were both reasoning from a partial picture.
opportunity: unified ingestion across wearables, lab reports, and chat
multi-model AI for typed reasoning over each modality
full production launch as a single-engineer scope
decisions: Sole-engineer scope demanded brutal cuts to the v1 surface.
Each modality processed by the model best suited to it — not one general LLM bolted to everything.
Recommendations always traced back to the input signal that produced them.
Designed for an actual launch, not a prototype demo.
experience: Blood report parsing and trend tracking.
Conversational interface over a user’s full health context.
impact: Shipped to production as sole engineer end-to-end.
Demonstrated multimodal AI in a real consumer health context.
Validated the full lifecycle — product design, architecture, build, launch — under solo scope.
reflection: Multimodal isn’t a feature — it’s an honesty filter. The moment three sources disagree, you discover what your product is really for.
